speaker: interviewer
text: It's like we were 15.

speaker: interviewee
text: He still is. Not really.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE] a piece of tape?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the little drawer by the phone.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: You know where you are, right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hm?

speaker: interviewer
text: You know where you are in the story, right? OK, good. That's what I tried, when I asked you about living in the cave, like I was trying to get you to talk more about-- you ready?

speaker: interviewer
text: Just about. OK. You know, other things at that time period. Because when--

speaker: interviewee
text: Was it OK?

speaker: interviewer
text: No, it was fine.

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean, it was those of interest? Anything?

speaker: interviewer
text: I just wanted to know-- yeah. No, I just wanted to know if there were more things you wanted to talk about from that period.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: But I want you to continue with the story where you were.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, you don't want me to go back to the caves?

speaker: interviewer
text: No.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, let's finish this story right now.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: But then we can go back to the caves at [? Benoit. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I want to tell you something.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is Cleveland, March-- or April 16th, 1995. We're with Betty Gold. This is tape number four. [? We're ?] [? speeding. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, when we saw those horses approaching us with my brother on one of them, we realized that the Ukrainians, the Germans found them and forced them to lead-- lead them to us. And again, we said goodbye to each other. This is the end, you know? We were glad. We really didn't even care anymore. We really didn't care. We decided, as soon as Bernie comes, my brother, we're all gonna start running. You plan one thing, you know, doesn't mean that it would work out. But that's what we decided. And we kissed each other goodbye again. And it turned out to be the partisans, the Russian underground. They are the one who discovered-- we thought it was the Germans when we saw the horses and the soldiers. It was the underground, Russian underground.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, what can I tell you? Liberation has come. I have never seen such tears, and-- and laughter, and screaming, and hysterics. I-- I can't begin to tell you. They tore their hair out, the adults. There was such mixed emotions of happiness and sadness, sad for those who died, and happy for those who survived. And you could just feel that. It was-- and I used to look up to them and say, how-- how can parents handle this? I mean, how-- how did we survive it? Already, at that time, I didn't believe it that-- that we're still alive. We had to pinch each other to believe it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, it was the Russian underground. And eventually, they took us with them. And we joined the partisans.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this exactly? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was, like, at the end of '44. Would that be right? I think it was the end of '44, or in the middle '44, end of the summer, or sometime during the summer of '44. And the underground found us. And they took us.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I don't if I can refer back to the caves or not. But let me tell you this then. When I was telling you, when my brother brought those seven breads and my father built a shelf, I was the one lying next to the shelf. Need I tell you more? I was the mouse who picked on the bread and stole the little pieces and sucked them like on a lollipop. And I picked, and I picked, and I picked, and I picked. And the next day, they discovered that there was a mouse in the cave. And they got me to admit that I was there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, instead of letting me sleep there, I was moved over on the other end, away from the bread. [LAUGHS] And they had an adult sleeping next to the bread, so the children wouldn't steal it. And that bread, I want you to know, lasted over a month. I don't know how they rationed it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did they keep it fresh? Or did you just--

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't keep it fresh. It was moldy, probably. It was probably-- who cared? It lasted about a month. And they gave us, every day, a little piece. They would break off and give-- give-- keep us alive with that little piece of bread. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] That's another incident. And when the Russians found us, like I said, there was joy, and sorrow, and tears, and whatnot. But the worst thing was-- and I'm going to go back a bit-- when we came back-- when we came back-- when we were the first killing of the first-- and we came back to the ghetto, my cousin's children were still alive. Two boys were still alive. They were with us in the wall. They were still alive.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my parents, and they, and us, we lived in a house, maybe with 60 people, you know, in-- in the second ghetto. It was Yom Kippur. I-- I forgot to tell you this. And they surrounded that little ghetto Yom Kippur morning, because they figured everybody who survived in the woods, if they were going to come in, they were going to come for Yom Kippur to pray. And they were right. Everybody-- most everybody came back. And we did too, at that time, when we were lost.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when they surrounded that ghetto again-- and we were going-- ran away to the woods-- they surrounded that ghetto that Yom Kippur morning, and everybody had to leave the houses. And they were killing all the-- took them all to one cave and killed them all.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, how did we get out of it? My father, and my cousin, his wife-- his cousin and his wife-- threw us out the window, the children first. And they told us, run, and we'll meet there and there again. And we ran. And they were the last to get out of the window, after the children were thrown out.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, my cousin and her husband had three-- two children, OK, there. And my parents had three children. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Her kids were gunned down and killed. We survived. On the way to the woods, I stepped over my uncle's body, bleeding to death. I stepped over my cousins' bodies. I stepped over my girlfriends' bodies. I stepped over a rabbi's body. Not only I-- this is what I did, but everybody else also. That's how I ran, over dead bodies, bleeding to death. And some of them screamed out to help them. And my father was yelling. Run! Run! Run! Run! And they're-- they're dying, or they're half dead, or they're wounded.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and you're-- what kind of animals are we? We're running to save our lives. Why? How can you watch some of your own flesh and blood dying and continue to run? My father kept yelling, run, run, run. Don't stop. And we ran. Whoever got shot down, dead. Whoever didn't, survived again for a while. And her two kids-- we had to go back in the caves with them and live with them. And my parents' children survived, and their-- none of theirs survived. And do you know how we felt? How guilty you feel? How sorry you feel? Your heart aches.

speaker: interviewee
text: And those parents look at us three kids, and their kids are dead. And we're alive. And it was a tremendous trying time. It was awful. It was just-- I-- I was wishing I was dead. I felt so sorry for them. I was wishing that-- I would have given my life for one of their kids to survive. Why did all three of us have to survive and her two kids-- her three kids, actually-- one died before. Choked. Why did all three of her children survive and-- us-- and her kids died. And I was pray-- I was so sorry that some of us didn't die and let one of her kids, or two of her kids live. No control. Nothing you can do. And I don't know where-- where people have the strength to-- to handle it all and the willpower to live. You hold onto a straw. It's ridiculous. I mean, big deal. So many people died, it would have been easier to die with them than to suffer the way we suffered.

speaker: interviewee
text: And why did we want to live? I don't know. There was no future. There was nothing to live for. I-- I don't understand it. I never will, of course. But except, like, the will to live is-- is impossible to describe. Impossible.

speaker: interviewee
text: Today, you can commit suicide legally. But there, people didn't want to do it. They had to wait until Hitler came and killed them. So it was a very trying time, very, very trying throughout the years. And we were a close family, and had to be-- that's the way it was. We're still very close with the parents. The father died with those kids. The mother's alive in Israel. My cousin, I'm very, very close with her. As a matter of fact, I'm her daughter now. I'm the only survivor right now, practically. Of all my family, I'm the last one. I'm the only one from the shtetl, from my family. Nobody else. It's scary. I don't know how I made it, but here I am. Anyway, when we were in the underground--

speaker: interviewer
text: So the Russians got you?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Russians got us. And we were just-- I don't know where they were marching. They were marching, and marching, and marching. We marched with them. We had no shoes. We had no clothes. And it was horrible. They would raid the Ukrainian villages, and take over their houses, and stay there. And we schlepped along. I didn't know where I was and what I was doing. Some had horses, some didn't. I couldn't walk. I wanted to be left behind. And somebody gave me a horse. And they put me on a horse. And I fell asleep on the horse. And they strapped me with a rope to the horse. And they gave me a little break, and I was able to ride a horse for a few miles. There was mud, I remember, and the soldiers, and the shootings. And I don't even know what I was doing there. I swear. And then they would break camp in the woods and set up tents. And the men would go and-- and bomb bridges. And-- and I don't know what else they did-- and railroad tracks, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: How was your father involved?

speaker: interviewee
text: Excuse me?

speaker: interviewer
text: How was your father involved with this? Did he--

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was-- was a soldier. He was a partisan. He had a-- even my brother, who was only two years older than me, they gave him a rifle. And he was a soldier. My mother was the cook. She was a cook and one of-- took care of, maybe, 25 or 30 soldiers she cooked for. And I was my mother's helper.

speaker: interviewee
text: So wherever they broke ground, we just kept following, and following, and following them. And the men were fighting, and the women were in the background helping with other duties, you know, washing clothes, and cooking, and so forth. And we-- it lasted like this till-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I don't know how long we were in the partisan. I can't remember. Maybe it was six months.

speaker: interviewee
text: And after that, they sent my father and my brother-- my older brother who was in the other cave, they found him too-- they sent him to the Russian Army, to Russia on the front. And they sent my brother on the front. I'm sorry. He was already, what, about 20 years old. And they sent my father to Kiev to work in a war factory, or whatever. And they separated us. And my mother and I were on a [NON-ENGLISH], which means a cooperative farm. And we were digging potatoes or planting potatoes and working in the fields. And very little to eat. And maybe 50 people in a house. And there was one little stove. And by the time it came your turn to cook a potato, you fell asleep. It was one toilet, maybe, for everybody to share, or one outhouse. It was a horrible, horrible, horrible, hungry, poor, very, very difficult life away from people, just the two of us some among all those Ukrainians in the field of Russians.

speaker: interviewee
text: And even then I sensed an anti-Semitism, you know? They were like-- they looked at us as certain creatures that came out of the-- like monkeys out of the woods or out of the jungle. Who are you? Where were you? Where did they find you? And they looked down on us. And they took advantage. And they laughed at us and made fun at us. Some of the ignorant peasants, especially, and the beer drinkers and those. So that-- that was very, very, very, very difficult. And then, it was almost towards the end of the war. And I remember, we were in Zhytomyr. And I remember, the bombs were still falling. But we didn't know where my father was.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my mother decided that I'm gonna go and find my father. So if I was in the partisans, I had a partisan hat with a little red ribbon, which indicated that I'm a partisan. So she-- she sent me to the railroad station, and she told me to take a trains, and to go to Kiev and find my father. I was-- I don't know how I was. 12, 11, I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: You must have been about 15-- 15 at this point.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I wasn't 15 yet.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because it was 1944.

speaker: interviewee
text: 14 or something?

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were born in 1930.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, about 14 or--

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were 14 or-- yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: 14, Or something like that. I got to the station, to the nearest station in Zhytomyr. And they only had freight trains. They didn't even have passenger trains. And most of the tracks were bombed, so they only had maybe one or two tracks. You had to wait hours and hours even to get a freight train. Finally, I found a freight train. And I sat on the steps in the train, because it was jammed full inside. Couldn't get in. I sat on the steps outside on the train, holding onto the rail-- two railings. And the wind was cutting my face to pieces. And I was so cold. And that took me to-- I don't know how many miles. And then, I stayed overnight, sleeping in some railroad tracks. And then I grabbed another train. And it took me to Kiev, also on the steps outside of the train. It was going, I don't know, 100 miles an hour, or whatever. But I was-- if I would have let go, I would have been killed in a minute, the wind was so strong. And I came to Kiev. I didn't know where to look. I didn't know. My father worked for an army place. And I started out with-- with military offices. And I started asking about my father, if anybody heard of him. Nobody-- nobody heard of him. Nobody knew of him. And night-- night approached, the first day. And I slept in a park. And then, the next morning, I started searching again. And I walked from place to place, to offices. Wherever I saw a flag, I figured it must be an important office. I would walk in and ask. Or military places, or if I see soldiers. And another day went by. And then I met some nice lady. And took me in her house, and fed me, let me sleep over. And on the third day I went again. And somebody told me there's a certain-- I told them my father used to work on leather goods, shoes, and briefcases, and all that. And maybe they have a place where they work with leather here, or something. And somebody told me, yeah. Yeah, there is. And I proceeded to go there. And I passed through a park. I sat down for a little while. And there was a little man next to me. And I spoke Russian. And we started. And he asked me what I'm doing here, a little girl alone, and all that.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I had a little bag on my-- I don't know, maybe a piece of bread, or whatever, or something to wear-- on my shoulder. And I told him I'm looking for my father. And in the meantime, my mother now-- doesn't know what's going on. There's no communication. And do you know something? Another miracle. He took me to my dad. That's how I found my father.

speaker: interviewee
text: He took me to my father, and we had a wonderful reunion. And then, I went back, also the same way as I came. And he got permission to go back on the [NON-ENGLISH] to be with his wife and children. And that's how I found my dad and brought him-- I didn't bring him back, but he came back afterwards.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was just a kid. I don't know, you know? Kiev was three million people. And I'm tramping around looking for Eli Potash. I mean, come on. [LAUGHS] I wouldn't even do it now. And it's easier now. But that was-- you know, so many miracles happened. You-- you just can't figure it out. You can't even begin to understand why and what. There's not-- nothing makes any sense, really and truly.

speaker: interviewee
text: And months later, my dad joined us. And eventually, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we-- there was a time-- we still lived at the [NON-ENGLISH] until there was a time when the war was almost over. It must have been almost over, or over, maybe. And they passed a law that any Polish people, citizens, can go back to Poland if they want.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my dad, my mother, and my brother, came back to-- there was no shtetl to come back to, because, as the last time we left the shtetl, it was burned to the ground. And we were in the woods watching burn our house and everybody else's homes. All the homes were burned to the ground that night when we ran away. Was this right at the beginning?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. It was like the second time, the second ghetto, the second time. They burnt every house, because they were all wooden houses. They burnt every single house. You don't know-- there's wilderness right there. You can't-- you don't know if there was a shtetl there at all. And the Nazis burned it down to the ground. And the Ukrainians helped them. And we watched it burn. And the flames-- it was just like watching the Civil War in Atlanta when was made. It was incredible, those flames. I'll never forget it as long as I live. It was just terrible for me. It was my home, my kids, my school, my toys, whatever, my doll, everything went up in-- the whole city, everything that meant anything to me went up in flames. I-- I will never forget those-- those flames. They were miles, miles high, and smoke. And the smell. The-- all the woods in the entire world smelled with smoke that night and that day. It was-- I've never seen such fires. I've never seen anything like it, even in the movies during wars that I witnessed. And I mean, to see our whole town burning-- your town, your home, you know, that's like the end of you. There's no one way-- there's no way ever see life again. And so there was no shtetl to come back to, so we went to the next shtetl, which was called Kiwerce. It wasn't far from Lutsk. And a few survivors, like my cousins that were hiding out with us, came there, and some other survivors, and a few more people. And we stayed there. We lived there. And at that time, the Russians were, of course, already came back. Russians [? took. ?] It was the end of the war, and so the Russians were there. And I don't remember what they did, how they made a living, but they worked. I remember the government gave them jobs or public offices and factories, or whatever. The men started to work, and we started to eat food. I mean, it was something else. But all of us, all of us, got deathly ill once we started to eat. Boils. I think-- I think, if I go to a physician right now with any of the boils that I had then, with one boil, whether it was on my arm, my breast, my neck, my butt, my legs, they would have amputated that part. That's how bad they were.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were told it's from the reaction, you know? Our bodies just couldn't handle all these foreign things in our system. We were so sick, all of us. The entire family bleeding to death practically. That's how it just poured out, all the poisons. For months, and months, and months, it was very, very difficult.

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean, here we have food, and we're not allowed-- we can't even eat it again because we're sick, we're so sick. Well, we didn't realize that, you know, you start little by little, a little water, a little this. We just started eating, you know, bread and potatoes-- we couldn't stop-- and vegetables, whatever we could find. It really made us deathly ill. And we stayed there [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] until-- I don't know how it worked, but I remember we left Kiwerce, and our destination was to get to Poland, to Germany, to Czechoslovakia. And there was some organization. And I really don't remember what organization.

speaker: interviewer
text: Maybe it was the Joint.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a Jewish organization.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it the Joint?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it wasn't. The Joint took care of us in the camps, once we got there. But this was the organization who used to steal refuges through the borders.

speaker: interviewer
text: The [? Irgun. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. Thank you. They came and rescued us. And they took us through the boundaries. And we traveled to-- to-- we traveled as Greeks. I remember, we weren't allowed to speak any-- nothing, any language, not Russian, Polish, Yiddish, Hebrew, nothing. Not a sound, because we were supposed to be Greeks. And they were representing us. And I had no idea where they were taking us. We were climbing on a-- trains, and then mountains, and then it's like-- then we finally got to Poland, you know like a--

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, another [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: Lodz. OK? We were writing a cattle train to get to Lodz. I don't know why Lodz, but I suppose that's what we're supposed to go. And on the cattle train, we were packed like, god knows, maybe 100 people in one car. And there was nothing to eat there. Nothing. There were raw potatoes. And my mother was sitting next to a couple of Gentile ladies. And the Gentile ladies were telling each other their stories. And my mother listened to it. And they had food on them. And she-- she got up and she said, you know, for a half a bread, I will tell you your fortune. She says, I'm a very famous fortune teller. My mother said that. We all look at her. She's crazy, or something.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she-- she overheard the conversation between them. And she takes her palm, and she tells her the story that she overheard, you know? Her aunt, her uncle, where she's going, where she comes from, something. They were amazed that she knew so much about them, so they all wanted their fortune told.

speaker: interviewee
text: This one offered a piece of bread, this one a potato, this one-- well, she didn't know anything about the other people. She says she-- she can't do it, because she can only do one a day. Otherwise, she has to concentrate 24 hours, till she gets her signals, so she can read somebody else's fortune. That's how we got food traveling on the trains. My mother was telling their fortunes. And on the way, my father-- the train stopped someplace. And my father had to go. And he went. He went to urinate. Well, he missed the train. It took a month for my father to find us again, because we took so many trains to get there. And then, we were-- we were heading already to Czechoslovakia. We were traveling on a train. And it only went so far, because the tracks were broken. And if we want to take another train, you're [INAUDIBLE]. You have to-- you're gonna have to wait here 24 hours. And on track number three, there might be a cattle train, or some train that might take us farther. I don't know where it came from and who had it, but there was a mattress, or inner spring, or something. We put it across the tracks, and seven people are lying down on the mattress to sleep a little bit. And the train comes. It throws the mattress off the tracks. Nobody got killed. My mother flew, maybe-- I don't know how many feet threw her high. Everybody. We were-- what happened is they hit the mattress, and the mattress kept flying with the people on it. This one fell here. This-- my mother was injured the most. All of us got very injured. Nobody got killed. Another miracle. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And from there, we got-- and the feathers started to fly and whatnot. And from there, we gathered again. We're waiting for-- I don't know. We walked on foot someplace. I don't remember. We walked on foot for many, many, many miles. And I remember we got to Czechoslovakia. I mean, I'm telling this, but this took weeks and months maybe. I don't remember exactly how long. And we got to Czechoslovakia. And they took us to the-- it was a famous building. It's called the [? Rothchild ?] Building. And there must have been thousands of refugees in that building, sleeping on the floors, sleeping on the walls and on the roofs. And don't ask. The sanitary conditions were incredible. I mean, it was smell. I threw up once, just from the smell. It was awful. We were sleeping on top of each other, like-- like animals. Like animals. And we had to live like this, I remember, for about a week or two, until we could get to Germany from there. Not to Germany, to Austria. From there, we got to Austria. Now, once we got to Austria, life began.

speaker: interviewer
text: I think you've left something out. I just want to-- about your brother.

speaker: interviewee
text: Could be. Oh. Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the meantime, while we were in Russia on the [NON-ENGLISH], my father in Kiev, my brother was in the army. They sent him in the army. And he-- we didn't know what happened to him after the war. We really didn't, until--

speaker: interviewer
text: Which brother is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: My oldest brother. We didn't know what happened to him or my cousins that were in the cave with him that were rescued by the partisans. We didn't know. We got separated. We-- we didn't hear of them. Then, after the war, we found out that the partisans sent him-- that the partisans sent him to Russia. And from Russia, he went-- they sent him to the front as a soldier. And he got killed on the front. And I think that was like four months before the war ended. And then, when we got to--

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were taken to Austria to a DP camp, was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: To a DP camp. And life began.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when did you-- how long did you remain in the DP camp? And what was the name of it?

speaker: interviewee
text: The name of the camp was Bindermichl.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was it located exactly?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Austria-- Linz, city of Linz. And there were many, many refugees, and many people who escaped the concentration camps, and some people who escaped in the woods, or in hiding with Gentiles, and from all sorts and walks of lives, and from all cities and countries. And it became like a small, little, tiny Jewish community with-- with I don't know how many buildings, maybe 20, 25 buildings, and maybe four or five, six families lived in a little suite. And we shared our-- we stood in line, and the UNRRA gave us food, and clothing, and shelter. And we would stand in line every day in soup kitchens, or whatever. But it was like-- I don't know-- like everybody came from the dead. It was like a celebration of life. It was such-- of course, I was a youngster. And I found some kids my own age. And believe it or not, we-- we had a social hall. We had dances. We had music. We had boys and girls. They gave us-- They started to teach us. School. In the camp, temporarily, they had a little schoolhouse. They had-- I went to a movie, I remember. I-- I've skipped so many things. On the trip, we got injured. We got hurt. We got broken bones and whatnot. But it was beginning like-- like the sun started to shine. And-- and we started to pick our pieces up. And everybody became family. You didn't have to be an aunt, an uncle, a cousin. Everybody was family. But there was a lot of sadness. We would take the bus to go to Linz. And especially the young Jewish survivors, young men, they were angry. They were bitter. They were rightly so. And I have witnessed where they would, you know, kill some-- brutally kill some Germans, and if they were certain that they were Nazis and they participated and killed somebody. They were recognized. Certain Nazis were recognized. And some of our Jewish people really took a hold of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm gonna-- we're gonna have to stop and change the tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: You know, I interviewed three men recently, all who were in Bindermichl.
